content.headline: A broken vinaigrette or sauce isn't a failure in technique — it's fat and water doing what they naturally do, and an emulsifier is the thing that stops them.
points: An emulsifier — mustard, egg yolk, or the proteins in cold butter — coats fat droplets and holds them suspended in liquid
Add oil slowly at first: a thin stream or drop by drop until the emulsion builds, then you can speed up
Butter sauces break above about 160°F — keep heat low and add cold butter off the heat or on barely-warm heat
A broken sauce is almost always fixable: start a new emulsifier base and slowly whisk the broken sauce back in
content.tell_me_more: Mayonnaise is a useful model for understanding all emulsions. One egg yolk contains enough lecithin — the emulsifier — to hold up to a cup of oil in suspension. That's extraordinary. The reason it works is physical, not chemical: the lecithin molecule has one end that's attracted to water and one end that's attracted to fat, so it literally bridges the gap between them. Mustard works similarly, though less powerfully. This is why even a tiny amount of mustard in a vinaigrette makes it last minutes longer before separating — the emulsifier buys time for the fat droplets to stay coated.

missions.title: Vinaigrette from Scratch
missions.description: Make a vinaigrette using 1 tablespoon Dijon mustard, 2 tablespoons red wine vinegar, and 4 tablespoons olive oil. Add the oil drop by drop at first while whisking vigorously, then in a thin stream once the emulsion begins to build.
missions.why_it_matters: A vinaigrette is the simplest possible emulsion — once you feel it come together, you understand every other emulsion.
missions.prompt: Show the finished vinaigrette in a bowl or jar immediately after making it. It should look creamy and slightly opaque — not separated into two distinct layers of oil on top and vinegar on the bottom. Tilt the bowl or jar to show that it pours as a unified, slightly thick liquid.
reflection_choices: Emulsion formed and held — sauce looks creamy and opaque, not separated
Emulsion formed but was thinner than expected — may need more mustard or more whisking
Broke before I could pour it — oil was added too fast at the start
Never quite came together — stayed oily with streaks rather than becoming creamy

missions.title: The Moment It Came Together
missions.description: Reflect on the point during whisking when you could tell the emulsion was actually forming.
missions.why_it_matters: Feeling and seeing the emulsion build in real time is what makes you able to recognize when it's about to break and catch it early.
missions.prompt: At what point during whisking did the texture of the vinaigrette change — was there a specific moment when it went from streaky to creamy? What did it feel like through the whisk when the emulsion built? And after it was done, how long did it stay emulsified before separating?
